perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it shall not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to any person, firm, or business, except to the extent necessary for the T ransaction.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it shall endeavor not to make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to any person, firm, or business, except to the extent necessary for the T ransaction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' weakens the obligation to protect Confidential Information. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt rather than a strict requirement, introducing ambiguity and making enforcement more difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in business. Weakening the obligation to protect confidential information could be viewed as an unfair practice, particularly if it harms the disclosing party. The modification introduces ambiguity, making it more difficult to prove a violation under Chapter 93A because it's unclear whether 'endeavor' constitutes a reasonable effort or a legal obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by changing "shall not" to "shall endeavor not to." This introduces ambiguity and makes enforcement more difficult. The Law Explanation suggests that this weakening could be viewed as an unfair practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, especially if it harms the disclosing party. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by outlining actions for unfair trade practices under Chapter 93A, Section 11, where any person suffering loss due to unfair practices can bring an action for damages. By weakening the obligation, the changed text increases the risk of such unfair practices occurring, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees disclose Confidential Inform ation only to those employees who need to know such information and certifies that such employees have previously agreed, either as a condition to employment or in order to obtain the Confidential Information, to be bound by terms and conditions substantia lly similar to those of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to generally limit disclosure of Confidential Information to employees with a need to know and believes that such employees have agreed to be bound by similar confidentiality terms.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires certification that employees are bound by similar terms. The change replaces 'certifies' with 'believes' and removes the requirement for prior agreement, creating ambiguity about the extent of the Receiving Party's responsibility and employee obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. The alteration introduces vagueness around the responsibility to ensure employee confidentiality, which could potentially violate this law if a breach of confidentiality occurs and causes harm due to inadequate safeguards. It shifts from a mandatory obligation ('certifies') to a subjective belief.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The modification from 'certifies' to 'believes' and the removal of the requirement for employees to have 'previously agreed' introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's responsibility to ensure employee confidentiality. This directly undermines the intent of M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts in trade or commerce, as cited in both scraped law snippets. By weakening the obligation to ensure employees are bound by confidentiality agreements, the revised contract creates a scenario where inadequate safeguards could lead to a breach, potentially violating the law.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confident ial Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive and other equitable relief (without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party may request injunctive and other equitable relief as deemed appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be entitled' to 'may request' diminishes the Disclosing Party's right to injunctive relief, making it discretionary rather than guaranteed. Removing the phrase '(without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages)' makes the process of obtaining injunctive relief subject to standard legal burdens, contradicting typical confidentiality agreement enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 65
perturbation.law_explanation: Mass. R. Civ. P. 65 governs the procedure for obtaining injunctive relief. The original text specifies the waiver of certain requirements to obtain injunctive relief, where as the modified text would require the disclosing party to adhere to typical burdens of proof for injunctive relief, thereby requiring them to satisfy the elements laid out under Rule 65, which is more difficult and time consuming.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be entitled' to 'may request' alters the Disclosing Party's right to injunctive relief from guaranteed to discretionary. Additionally, removing '(without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages)' shifts the burden to the disclosing party, making it more difficult to obtain injunctive relief as highlighted in the law explanation. The original text waived certain requirements to obtain injunctive relief. The modified text would require the disclosing party to adhere to typical burdens of proof for injunctive relief, which is more difficult. This creates a contradiction by weakening the protections afforded to the disclosing party in the original contract.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Massachusetts and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state an d federal courts sitting in Boston , Massachusetts .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally guided by the principles of law in the State of Massachusetts and both parties acknowledge the potential jurisdiction of courts in Boston, Massachusetts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be construed and controlled' to 'shall be generally guided' weakens the governing law provision. Replacing 'consent to jurisdiction' with 'acknowledge the potential jurisdiction' makes the forum selection clause non-binding and ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 allows civil actions for unfair or deceptive acts or practices in business. Making the governing law and jurisdiction ambiguous could be seen as an unfair practice if it hinders a party's ability to seek redress under this statute. This is because it becomes unclear where and how disputes will be resolved, creating uncertainty and potential hardship for the aggrieved party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures Massachusetts law governs and consents to jurisdiction in Boston courts. The changed text weakens this by stating the agreement is 'generally guided' by Massachusetts law and acknowledges 'potential jurisdiction.' This creates ambiguity about which laws apply and where disputes can be resolved. The Law Explanation argues this hinders seeking redress under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, which allows civil actions for unfair business practices. Scraped Law Snippet 1 details the right to bring action in superior court for unfair trade practices, highlighting the importance of a clear jurisdictional agreement. The change undermines this right by creating uncertainty, potentially contradicting the law's intent to provide accessible legal recourse.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confide ntial Information at Disclosing Party’s request, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will, when practical, return available originals of Confidential Information, or may consider certifying destruction of the same.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries' to 'will, when practical, return available originals' and 'at Disclosing Party's option, certify destruction of the same' to 'may consider certifying destruction of the same', the obligation to return or destroy all confidential information becomes vague and discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), M.G.L. c. 93 § 42
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), M.G.L. c. 93 § 42 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of potential misappropriation, undermining the protections afforded by MUTSA. If the Receiving Party doesn't return or destroy all confidential information, the Disclosing Party might have a harder time preventing potential future misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, potentially increasing the risk of misappropriation. The original text mandates the return or destruction of 'all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries,' whereas the changed text states the Receiving Party 'will, when practical, return available originals' and 'may consider certifying destruction.' This shift from a mandatory to a discretionary action directly undermines the protection against trade secret misappropriation. Snippet 1 defines misappropriation as the acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret by improper means or without consent. The modification weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to prevent such misappropriation because the Receiving Party now has more discretion in handling confidential information.
